data.title: OSI Systems: Backlog And Security Tailwinds Drive Momentum (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of OSI Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:OSIS) stock, it has gained nearly 22% in price.

OSI Systems has experienced strong momentum, driven primarily by robust growth in its Security division. With record Q2 FY25 results leading to upward revisions to guidance, the market has become rationally bullish on the stock recently. OSI's unprecedented $1.8 billion backlog reflects strong demand visibility and ongoing international expansion. When these wins are coupled with favorable geopolitical tailwinds due to OSI's ability to capitalize on growing global security infrastructure needs, the stock appears to be a clear Buy. However, beware of an elevated valuation with already high expectations from the market; the potential normalization of growth rates could temper investor enthusiasm in the medium term.

Operational & Fundamental Analysis

OSI operates across three divisions. The first of these is Security, which is the majority of its revenues, where it designs and manufactures security screening products and services globally. One of its key differentiators is its ability to provide turnkey security screening solutions, not just standalone scanners. Its Healthcare division is smaller, and operates under the Spacelabs Healthcare brand, where its monitors track vital signs for patients in critical and perioperative care. Finally, the company's Optoelectronics and Manufacturing division, which is larger than the Healthcare division but still substantially smaller than Security, gives the company a unique vertical integration. Through this division, the company designs and produces optoelectronic devices, printed circuit boards, and provides electronics manufacturing services ("EMS"); it serves both various end markets and its own Security and Healthcare products, and it differentiates itself through highly customizable solutions.

In January, the company delivered record Q2 FY25 earnings, and following that it raised full-year guidance. Key highlights include its record Security division backlog. This outlook ties in with broader company momentum and contract wins, particularly in its high-weight Security segment. FY25 guidance has actually increased twice so far, after Q1 and Q2. The positive revisions are fundamentally underpinned by OSI's execution. Management has demonstrated an ability to direct the company to convert its backlog to revenue efficiently. For example, it has ramped a major security contract in Mexico and been able to secure new deals to replenish that backlog.

In total, the backlog now sits at an unprecedented ~$1.8 billion in orders as of the last quarter; this is roughly equal to an entire year's revenue, providing a high degree of visibility for future sales. In Q2 FY25, OSI reported a book-to-bill ratio of 1.2. In particular, a hefty backlog in the Security division acts as a growth engine, and this should translate into double-digit revenue growth over the next 1-2 years as it is converted to sales.

Government agencies are currently investing heavily in security infrastructure. In the past year, OSI secured numerous contracts, including a $100 million award for cargo and vehicle inspection solutions in March 2024, a contract to supply advanced CT baggage scanners at a major European airport, and a $59 million order for cargo inspection systems in January 2024. It also won a 10-year security screening services contract in Uruguay and various mid-sized orders in the Middle East and Asia. Therefore, international expansion represents a substantial growth opportunity. New product launches like next-generation explosive trace detection ("ETD") systems also provide broader growth momentum. The company is benefiting from broader growth trends in the technology and security markets, a trend that is heightened given current geopolitical tensions and the outbreak of hot wars in the Middle East and Eastern Europe. In essence, these dynamics open up OSI stock as a momentum/growth play with secular tailwinds. OSI's security division grew by 27% year-over-year in Q4 FY24, closing out the last full financial year robustly.

While the Security division is particularly shining right now, OSI's Optoelectronics and Manufacturing division has also contributed positively, benefiting from increased manufacturing business. However, it may be subject to heavy tariffs as it has an international manufacturing presence, including in the United Kingdom, Mexico, India, Malaysia, and Indonesia. It also manufactures in the U.S., but expect margin pressures in the short to medium term as the company adapts to new trade barriers and further regionalizes production to fine-tune high margins from demand. The Healthcare division, while facing pressure due to financial challenges for hospitals, has shown signs of improvement, including securing a $4 million contract for patient monitoring solutions.

Price & Valuation Analysis (Including Risks)

Although OSI is benefiting from strong secular tailwinds, powerful product positioning, and elevating guidance, the stock price already reflects this fully. We can see evidence of this in the way the price has elevated above the 50-week moving average substantially. Only recently, during the broader market sell-off related to international trade war concerns, has OSI stock corrected significantly. However, this is not necessarily a buying opportunity; instead, it's a risk-on bet for alpha generation in a market with a heightened risk of recession right now.

There is a risk that market momentum is overweighting the company's medium-term growth prospects. A significant portion of OSI's growth has been driven by a catch-up in orders post-pandemic and a handful of very large contracts. The Mexico program in particular is past its peak, according to comments from the recent earnings calls, so if new orders don't match the size of these peak programs, OSI's organic growth could decelerate. There is a risk that 2024-2025 represents a high-water mark in growth that isn't maintained. In addition, government projects are inherently lumpy, and prone to delays. This reliance on government and defense spending also opens the company up to heightened policy risk, which could weigh on future growth and lead to a sentiment reversion, which is currently not indicated in forward guidance and current fundamentals. I think investors might be overlooking how cyclical and policy-dependent some of OSI's demand is, instead treating the backlog as if it will perpetually replenish itself.

Furthermore, as the company's normalized trailing P/E ratio is about 20, which is still higher than its five-year average of 18, the valuation assumes flawless execution. Forward diluted EPS growth is pegged at 18%, with 19.3% being the company's five-year trailing average. Therefore, this allocation requires a degree of caution. I am bullish, but reservedly so. An estimated $11 in TTM normalized EPS in April 2027 leads to a stock price of $220 if the forward P/E in one year is 20, slightly expanded due to major macro concerns likely abating by then. At the J.P. Morgan Industrials Conference on March 11, 2025, OSI Systems' Chief Financial Officer, Alan Edrick, addressed concerns regarding potential tariff impacts on the company. He stated that they will not significantly impact OSI, because the company has a diversified international production base to mitigate risks associated with region-specific tariffs by reallocating production as needed.

My $225 12-month price target for OSIS stock indicates a 28.5% upside from the present price of $175. However, due to the nature of the business (i.e., a mid-cap vulnerable to changes in policy, government contracts, and changes to industry tailwinds) my rating is a Buy and not a Strong Buy.

Upcoming Earnings

Q3 FY25 results are approaching, and the company is still likely to post solid growth, albeit at a lower rate to Q3 FY24's blockbuster 34% revenue growth as large contracts ramped. Around 10% year-over-year revenue growth seems about right for OSI in Q3 FY25. Most importantly, investors will scrutinize the order pipeline. Any update on backlog (which was >$1.8 billion last quarter) will be key. If it stays flat or increases, it signals strong ongoing demand. Look for whether management are seeing continued international opportunities or any slowdown in government decision-making. Moreover, make sure the book-to-bill ratio is over 1. These factors could set the stage positively for the rest of FY25 and the beginning of FY26. Historically, Q4 is OSI's strongest quarter, so any hints from management on how Q4 is shaping up would be valuable. Any signs of order slowdown or margin pressure would likely be met with a quick stock reaction due to the high expectations built into the price.

Conclusion: Buy

OSI Systems presents a compelling investment case due to its strong recent operational performance, record backlog visibility, and strategic positioning within critical global markets. Early signs of improvement in Healthcare and steady contributions from Optoelectronics add valuable diversification, although these segments are more vulnerable at the moment. The Security division is really where I expect the company to continue to shine as geopolitical conditions necessitate higher global security measures. However, investors need to be aware that the stock price is already elevated, and any downward revisions in the forward outlook from management in the coming quarters means that sentiment for the stock could quickly turn negative. Thankfully, management has expressed confidence in navigating tariff-related challenges due to its geographically diverse manufacturing footprint, but margin pressures in the short to medium term still remain a possibility. My 12-month price target of $225 suggests a significant 28.5% upside from the current level of $175, but risks related to cyclical demand and dependency on government spending limit my conviction to a Buy rather than a Strong Buy.

data.title: Levi Strauss & Co. Has Finally Gone On Sale, If Just Barely (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Levi Strauss overview

Some of the best buying opportunities in the market can be had when market conditions are extremely pessimistic. Absent some major political change, it appears to me as though we are almost certainly heading toward stagflation and a recessionary environment. This self-imposed pain should impact most companies in most industries. But the good news is that the panic that has ensued has opened up the door for bargain buying. One interesting candidate that investors can consider is jeans company Levi Strauss & Co. (NYSE:LEVI).

Back in April of last year, I decided to downgrade the business from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’. During the time that I was bullish on it, shares had risen by 58.8% at a time when the S&P 500 was up only 20.6%. This caused the company to no longer be attractively priced. Ultimately, I believed that downgrading it was appropriate. Since then, the stock has plummeted by 33.9% compared to the 1.9% decline experienced by the broader market. The vast majority of this downside occurred this year, with shares down 21.8% year to date. However, the business does now appear to be priced at levels that could offer an opportunity for investors.

This became apparent on April 7th after management announced financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Even though revenue fell short of analysts’ expectations, earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share both came in higher than expected. The fact that shares rose 7.4% in after-hours trading, even as management provided very little in the way of detail as to how recently announced tariffs would affect the company, is testament to the fact that shareholders are currently pleased with how things are going. Because of these factors, and because of how shares are currently priced, I believe that upgrading the company to a very soft ‘buy’ makes sense at this time.

A look at Levi's recent results

For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, Levi Strauss generated revenue of $1.53 billion. This represents an increase over the $1.48 billion reported the previous year, but sadly fell short of analysts’ expectations by $10 million. This rise in sales was driven mostly by the firm’s Levi’s Brands products, with revenue climbing by 3% from $1.45 billion to $1.49 billion. Actual organic revenue growth was 8.5% during this time. The difference can really be attributed to foreign currency fluctuations that negatively affected revenue by $46 million. The company also saw a 9.8% increase in sales associated with its Beyond Yoga brand, with revenue climbing from $32.1 million to $35.2 million. It is worth noting that the overall increase in sales for the company came in spite of the fact that it saw an $11.9 million hit in revenue associated with the sale of its Denizen business and a $16 million hit associated with the sale of its footwear category of products.

Geographically speaking, the firm saw growth in both the Americas and Asia. In the Americas, revenue rose by 6.4%, or 11.1% on an organic basis. Ultimately, the push in sales from $735.8 million to $783 million can be attributed to 45 new company-operated stores in operation compared to the same time the previous year, and because of greater e-commerce sales. In Asia, the firm saw a 6.7% rise in revenue, with that figure growing 10% on an organic basis. The rise in sales from $288.8 million to $308.1 million can really be attributed to 37 additional company-operated stores. It was really only in Europe that the business reported a decline in revenue, with sales falling 5.4% from $423.5 million to $400.5 million. The hit here was an $18 million impact associated with foreign currency fluctuations. However, the firm did offset this to some extent by an increase in store count of nine locations. On an organic basis, revenue grew by 2.9%.

On the bottom line, the company reported earnings per share of $0.35. This excludes a $0.01 per share loss resulting from discontinued operations. You see, in the final quarter of 2024, the company decided to look for strategic alternatives involving its Dockers brand. And as of the first quarter of this year, it decided that it would pursue a sale of that business. It only accounts for about 5% of overall revenue for the company. So I don't really expect it to have a big impact on results. But it does mean that prior year financial results are not exactly comparable. Anyways, the earnings per share from continuing operations in the first quarter of 2024 ended up being negative to the tune of $0.03, so the firm did see a nice year-over-year increase. Furthermore, the earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.07 greater than what analysts were expecting to see. This year-over-year improvement caused net profits for the company to go from $10.6 million to $135 million.

On an adjusted basis, earnings per share totaled $0.38. That compares favorably to the $0.25 per share reported the previous year and translates to a rise in adjusted profits from $100.4 million to $150 million. The adjusted earnings per share for the company ended up being $0.10 greater than what analysts were hoping to see. Most other profitability metrics followed suit. Operating cash flow was the exception, plunging from $286 million to $52.5 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $28.9 million to $217.3 million. Furthermore, EBITDA for the company expanded from $182.1 million to $253.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2023 and 2024 fiscal years. With the exception of net profits, you can see a year-over-year improvement across the board when it comes to revenue, profits, and cash flows. So clearly, the performance achieved in the first quarter of 2025 is part of a larger trend and not just a blip on the radar.

Management further expects organic revenue to rise by between 3.5% and 4.5% this year. However, because of the foreign currency fluctuations, they believe that GAAP net revenue will fall by between 1% and 2%. This is consistent with prior expectations that management announced when results for the final quarter of last year came out. The firm did increase its guidance when it comes to its EBIT margin, however, with an expected range now of 11.4% to 11.6%. This compares to the 10.9% to 11.1% previously anticipated. They also still expect earnings per share of between $1.20 and $1.25 on an adjusted basis. This is after adding back a $0.20 negative impact associated with foreign currency fluctuations and a higher tax rate for the year. Based on my own estimates, all of this translates to adjusted net profits of about $490.1 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $534.2 million, and EBITDA of $913.1 million.

If we use these estimates, as well as the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that range between the undervalued range and the low end of the fair value range, as I see it. Compared to other similar companies, however, shares are on the cheaper end of the spectrum. In the table below, I compared Levi Strauss to five similar enterprises. It is true that on a price to operating cash flow basis, the company ended up being more expensive than four of the five companies I stacked it up against. But when using the other two profitability metrics, only one of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Levi Strauss & Co. 10.9 10.0 7.0
Gildan Activewear (GIL) 15.7 12.7 10.1
V.F. Corporation (VFC) 58.9 8.1

25.3
Columbia Sportswear Company (COLM) 16.9 7.8 9.1
PVH Corp. (PVH) 6.3 5.1 4.6
Kontoor Brands (KTB) 12.8 8.6 9.4

It is important to be cognizant of the fact that the guidance provided by management does not factor in the new tariffs that have been imposed by the US and certain retaliatory tariffs that are relevant to the business. Management did say that they expect the impact for the second quarter of this year to be minimal. But beyond that, they are still evaluating what things will look like moving forward. They did acknowledge that the most recent round of tariffs ‘pose a significant challenge’ for the company and the industry it operates in. We do know that only about 1% of its products by revenue come into the US from China. From Mexico, that number is around 5%. But for Vietnam, which has been slapped with a 46% tariff, the figure is in the mid to high single digits. Other major countries that the company imports products from include Bangladesh, Cambodia, Egypt, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka. But the firm did not provide any estimates for those nations.

Takeaway

Right now, it is clear that we have some uncertain circumstances that we are dealing with. It would have been nice for management to give a more substantive assessment of the tariff situation. However, the company is clearly doing well if we ignore that shortcoming. Revenue, profits, and most cash flow figures are growing nicely. Shares have also become cheaper and are now attractively priced, especially relative to similar companies. Given these factors, I believe that the firm deserves an upgrade to a very soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: PayPal: This Selloff Is A Gift
data.description:
Intro

I have been interested in PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) ever since its valuation has dropped below a P/E of 20, which was in early 2023. Amid the current tariff selloff, its share price has now come down by 46% from its 2024 high, to similar levels of below $60 again. My ambition now is to create a comprehensive analysis of an investment into PYPL today.

PayPal is a digital payments platform that connects merchants with consumers. EPS growth has been weak recently, but the company is putting in some effort to make the turnaround work and has already improved some profitability metrics.

Despite minor risks of PYPL, such as being a value trap and its competition increasing, their growth opportunities are countless, leading to a Risk-to-Reward ratio among the best in today's market, which marks a strong buy for GARP investors like myself.

Recent performance

Recent financial data has been compelling. Here are some of the highlights of their Q4 2024 earnings presentation:

Total Payment Volume (TPV) increased by 10% YOY. Transaction Margin Dollars excluding interest on customer's balances (essentially PYPL's gross profit adjusted for interest income) increased by 5% YOY and non-GAAP operating margin increased by 116 basis points YOY, both showcasing improvements in profitability. They're guiding for at least 5% growth of TM$ ex. interest in 2025 again.

Valuation

Historically, PayPal almost trades at its cheapest valuation levels ever, and at a discount of 42% to the S&P 500.

PYPL's 2025 forward P/E is only 11.6, which is unheard of for a company of this quality and again historically low.

This shows just how discounted the stock is. The lower a stock's valuations are, the lower are the expectations of investors for future EPS growth. Thus, PYPL has much more potential to surprise positively than negatively. I outline how they can achieve this in the following.

Growth Drivers

The first growth driver for PayPal is the most obvious one. The trend of digital payments had growth numbers on steroids during Covid-19. This doesn't mean that growth is stagnating now, though, since the US digital payments market is still expected to grow at 21% p.a. PayPal makes about 56% of its revenues in the US. For PayPal, this should result in steady TPV growth.

An additional cause for solid TPV growth has been PayPal's strong brand reputation. They're very user-friendly, which I can certainly confirm, having experienced the two sides of PayPal, both as a consumer and using it for my business as well, and having never faced any serious issues.

Management said they're shifting their focus towards sustainable profitability over high revenue growth, which I believe to be the correct course of action. This includes renegotiating contracts for Braintree and cutting costs. Their employee count, for example, has been trending down, peaking at 31,000 in 2021 and currently sitting around 24,000.

Advertisements are a completely new way for PYPL to generate revenue, but they make total sense. They have just been introduced in the UK. While it's hard to predict, some analysts estimate this new business section to add around 5-10% of revenue per year and additionally boosting TM$ due to advertisement's high-margin nature.

PayPal has offered cryptocurrency payments and trading for some time now. They are continually developing their offerings, as they recently added major coins Chainlink and Solana to payments.

Most importantly and chiefly underappreciated on this aspect, however, is PYPL's own stablecoin PYUSD. Being 100% backed by cash (equivalents), with further cryptocurrency adoption and thereby stablecoin usage, PYUSD is well positioned to take some market share from giants like Tether or Circle. These companies are not publically traded and are therefore less transparent with their financials. Tether specifically has been doubted to own enough reserves for its 120 billion stablecoin USDT multiple times. It is not hard to imagine that investors are more likely to trust a more financially transparent, reputable brand to store their funds. Tether achieved a net profit of 13 billion in 2024. If PYPL could gain just 5% of that market, they would add $700 million to their bottom line.

Finally, it is difficult to predict which companies are set to benefit the most, or at all, from AI. However, the argument recently has been turning towards companies with a large customer base and huge amounts of customer data being the biggest beneficiaries of AI. Both is the case for PayPal, being this two-sided payment solutions company. They can leverage AI to personalize checkouts, show consumers more relevant ads or upsells, improve their fraud detection and cut costs on costly processes, significantly contributing to the top and bottom line.

Other positive aspects

The most obvious, and admittedly fairly often mentioned aspect, is their share repurchases. The lower the share price falls, the more effective buybacks become since management can reduce the share count faster, and in turn grow EPS quicker. At current prices, the $20 billion allocated to buy back shares, could theoretically purchase 35% of the whole company. However, they guided for $6 billion of share repurchases this year, which would still result in share count reduction by 10%, boosting EPS growth long term.

Additionally, PayPal has a rock solid balance sheet. I don't see the possibility of financial distress for PYPL, having been FCF positive for over 6 years. Their cash (equivalents) + short-term investments outweigh long-term debt by over one billion dollars. That's partly why I believe in the case of a recession, which has become much more likely this year, PayPal would be fine. That doesn't mean that earnings won't slump as consumer spending weakens, but they'll most likely not go bankrupt and survive a downturn.

In turn, when interest rates are lower, which in my opinion is only a matter of time with now 4 rate cuts expected this year, PayPal is set to profit exponentially from that. Being a FinTech company, they're traded like a risk asset, which outperforms when interest rates are low due to investors' rotation into stocks.

With their digital business model, PayPal is much less susceptible to the determined tariffs than other companies that have manufacturing in foreign companies that are being tariffed now, such as Nike (NKE). It still dropped more than the S&P 500 on this news, making it even more attractive.

Risks

The first and most obvious risk to my rationale about PayPal is simply that it poses as a value trap today. Their new growth opportunities don't work in their favor, and their core business grows very slowly. EPS growth for this company will remain flat or even decline, leading to significant shareholder value destruction. No amount of buybacks can save a company with shrinking earnings.

Another risk is the increasing threat by competitors Apple Pay, Block, and Stripe, which might have the resources to launch more innovative and competitive products than PayPal and gain market share over them. Such an environment might also lead to a decline of PayPal's take rates, further pressuring its margins.

Price targets

The goal now is to get to a price target, from which we can derive a prediction about PYPL's future returns. I aim to include all aspects mentioned above, but still estimate numbers conservatively enough to achieve a margin of safety. I will be calculating potential returns 5 years ahead, until 2029. I purposely neglect revenue and earnings estimates by analysts to create scenarios that I personally deem realistic.

Bear Case

Assumptions:

  • TPV and thereby PayPal's revenue growth will slow down to just 2% due to competition and/or economic weakness.
  • New measures will add zero growth to PYPL's top line.
  • Margins decrease to 11% due to intensified competition.
  • Multiples slightly contract to a P/E of 11.
  • Shares outstanding decrease by 2% per year.

In this case, 2029 revenue would hit $35.11 billion and net income $3.86 billion. Divided by 914 million shares outstanding, EPS would be $4.22. 2029 share price would reach $46.48, implying a -4.3% CAGR.

Base Case

Assumptions:

  • TPV grows at 5%.
  • 3% additional yearly revenue growth by new measures, leading to 8% revenue growth overall.
  • Net margins remain at 13%.
  • Slight multiple expansion occurs, to a P/E of 17.
  • Share count decreases by 3% per year.

Using these assumptions, PYPL's revenue would total $46.72 billion. Dividing net income of $6.07 billion by 870 million shares outstanding, EPS would reach $6.98 in 2029. Share price would therefore reach $118.7, which leads to a CAGR of 15.4%.

Bull Case

Assumptions:

  • TPV at 7%.
  • New measures add 7% p.a. in revenue growth, totalling 14%.
  • Net margins are slightly expanded to 15%.
  • The higher-than-expected EPS growth justifies a re-rating of PYPL and P/E is expanded to 22.
  • Share count shrinks by 4% every year.

Now, in this rather bullish scenario, PayPal would turn over $61.2 billion in 2029 and earn $9.18 billion of net income. Earnings per share would climb all the way to $11.16, resulting in a share price of $245.5 and a CAGR of 33.4%.

I believe the Base Case could prove realistic, which is why I am bullish.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Great buying opportunities arise when companies that should not be heavily affected by certain changes are sold off heavily by the announcement of such changes. This is what happened over the last 2 months for PayPal. One could make the case of PYPL being a value trap and increasing competition, but I believe the opportunities far outweigh the risks. Even though share prices could continue to drop, PayPal's Risk/Reward ratio is already impeccable today. If it drops even further, fundamentally all else being equal, I will simply add more.

data.title: 3 REITs To Sell Before They Cut Their Dividend
data.description:
Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH) recently cut its dividend.

Unfortunately, we did not see that one coming.

The management had recently made multi-million-dollar insider stock purchases, and the dividend was fully covered. The payout ratio was high in 2025 but is expected to come back down in 2026; therefore, we did not see a need for a cut. The board had even hiked the dividend just a year earlier.

But a new CEO came in and wants to refocus the strategy on retaining more cash flow for deleveraging and growth. In the long term, it is likely a good idea. But in the short term, the company is likely to face a poor market sentiment, with its share price crashing down to lower levels:

This is a good example of what typically happens when a REIT cuts its dividend.

Therefore, investors generally prefer to avoid REITs (VNQ) at high risk of cutting the cheese, as this would likely also lead to a sell-off, at least initially.

Here are three more popular REITs that, we think, are likely to cut their dividend in 2025 or 2026:

Global Medical REIT (GMRE)

We used to own Global Medical REIT at High Yield Landlord and think it is a great REIT overall.

They have a unique strategy focusing on higher-cap rate medical office buildings in secondary markets, and the management is well-aligned with shareholders.

But the issue is its balance sheet.

The REIT has quite a bit of debt, with its 50% LTV and 6.5x Debt-to-EBITDA.

Moreover, its debt maturities are relatively short at just 2 years on average, and the company isn't retaining enough cash flow to gradually pay off debt.

Today, its payout ratio is 98%, leaving no room for error.

So unless interest rates suddenly drop to much lower levels, I just don't see how the REIT will manage to maintain its dividend.

I would add that its strategy is also riskier than average, focusing on higher-yielding properties, and that will naturally also come with occasional setbacks.

GMRE had some exposure to Steward, for example, a tenant that went bankrupt.

All it would take is one small lease delinquency, and the dividend wouldn't be covered anymore.

Yet, GMRE is owned by a lot of income-oriented investors for its 11% dividend yield. As it cuts down its dividend, I expect its share price to drop quite a lot, and we are avoiding it for this reason.

Global Net Lease (GNL)

Global Net Lease is in a similar situation in that the REIT has too much leverage, short maturities, and doesn't retain enough cash flow to organically deleverage in the wake of rising interest expenses.

Its debt-to-EBITDA is about 7x, and its payout ratio is near 100% as well.

However, GNL is even worse than GMRE because its management has a very bad track record.

The company went public in 2016 and has yet to earn a positive return to its shareholders. They have done so poorly, in my opinion, because they have had many capital raises that were dilutive to shareholders, bought low-quality assets, and used too much leverage:

They have also cut the dividend many times over the years. Each time, they claimed they were now set to return to growth with a right-sized dividend, but yet another cut was always around the corner.

Again, today's payout ratio is unsustainable, and unless interest rates suddenly come back down to 0%, I think they will be forced to cut it to a lower level.

Either they cut it, or they put the entire company at risk.

Arbor Realty Trust (ABR)

Arbor Realty Trust is a very popular REIT on Seeking Alpha, and it is easy to see why.

The company offers a 15% dividend yield and has historically done very well for its shareholders.

It has managed to grow its FFO per share and hike the dividend year after year.

It is one of the most heavily shorted REITs today because its loan book is facing growing distress.

The mortgage REIT lent a lot of money to new multifamily development projects following the pandemic, when interest rates and cap rates were ultra-low.

But then interest rates surged, and so did cap rates. As a result, property values dropped, and increasingly, many of its loans became very risky.

A very vocal short seller called Fraser Perring claims that things are far worse than it seems:

"Arbor originated some of the most opportunistic loans in the multifamily space. If you believe Kaufman, ice doesn't melt. 90+% of ABRs book is credit watch &/or distressed. ABRs delinquencies worse."

The management admits to dealing with growing difficulties on their earnings call as they describe the current environment as "extremely challenging" and noted that they will "likely" need to adjust their dividend on their earnings call.

Here is what he said about the dividend:

"Based on these factors, we are now estimating our earnings for 2025 will be in the range of $0.30 to $0.35 a quarter and will likely reset our dividend starting in the first quarter of this year in accordance with this new guidance. This outlook is reflective of the newly elevated rate environment. However, if there is a material change in short-term or long-term rates in the future, we will revise our outlook accordingly.

It's important to note that we were the only firm in our peer group to set our dividend -- to grow our dividend over the last five years by 43%, while every other company in our space has cut their dividend some multiple times by 40% on average with only one company keeping their dividend flat in the last five years. And we assume that we reset our dividend to the midpoint of our new earnings guidance, our dividend will be up approximately 8%, which is again -- which again is compared to our peers who are down at an average of 40%."

The current dividend is $0.43 per quarter.

They talk about potentially cutting it to around $0.32, representing a 25% cut.

But I think they should cut it even lower, just in case things get tough, and we face a recession and/or another surge in inflation and rates.

I would not be surprised if they needed to cut the dividend a second time before eventually returning to growth.

Closing Note

Most REITs today continue to grow their dividend payments, but not all of them do so. With the sector facing growing uncertainty, now is the time to be more selective than ever.

data.title: The Dow-To-Gold Ratio: Is The Bubble About To Pop?
data.description:
By Mike Maharrey

Stocks have taken a beating over the last several days. The conventional wisdom is that the sell-off is due to worries about the impact of the Trump tariffs.

The aggressive tariff regime certainly triggered the sudden and sharp downturn in the stock market, but is something deeper going on?

I think there is.

The big sell-off in response to tariffs is just one chapter in a much longer novel. A look at a metric known as the Dow-to-gold ratio hints at the larger narrative.

In a nutshell, the stock market is historically overvalued. In other words, it is a bubble. And one thing we know about bubbles is that they eventually pop.

All it takes is a pin.

Tariffs might be the pin.

But even if stocks manage to regain their footing, another pin is out there.

What Is the Dow-Gold Ratio?

The Dow-to-Gold ratio prices the Dow Jones Index in ounces of gold. Currently, the ratio stands at just over 12.5-1. In other words, it would take around 12.5 ounces of gold to "buy" the Dow.

As you can see from the chart, the ratio is starting to break down.

What is this telling us?

Ratios are valuable in revealing trends and cycles. For instance, a large spread in the gold-to-silver ratio (such as the 100:1 ratio today) tells us that silver is significantly undervalued compared to gold. When we get these large spreads, ratios typically revert back to the mean. For instance, the gold-to-silver ratio fell to 30-1 in 2011 after rising to over 80-1 during the money creation of the Great Recession in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis.

We can track similar trends in the Dow-to-gold ratio.

What we find is that historically, a falling Dow-to-Gold ratio precedes or coincides with financial crises or long-term bear markets.

Going way back in time, the ratio fell from around 18-1 to 2-1 in the early years of the Great Depression.

During the stagflationary years of the '70s, stocks tanked, but gold took off on a long bull rally. In 1980, the Dow-Gold ratio briefly touched 1-1.

As we can see from the chart, stock valuations took off in the 90s. Between that low in 1980 to 2000, the index moved as high as 43-1.

How do we account for this massive runup in stocks?

There were certainly many factors, but one of the most significant drivers was money creation by the Federal Reserve that helped pump up the dot-com bubble. The extreme overvaluation of tech stocks drove the Dow-to-gold ratio to all-time highs.

And then the bubble popped.

The Fed managed to keep some air in the bubble for a while with a massive injection of easy money, but the bubble popped as we endured the 2008 financial crisis and the Great Recession. Between 2000 and 2011, the ratio fell to as low as 6:1.

And then the Fed went to work again.

The Dow-to-gold ratio peaked at over 18:1 in 2018, thanks to a decade of easy money. The ratio fell modestly in late 2018 as the stock market tanked and the central bank finally attempted to "normalize" monetary policy. But when the market crashed in the fall of that year, the central bank pivoted back to quantitative easing to keep the air flowing.

The pandemic gave the Fed cover to double down on loose monetary policy and blow even more air into the stock market bubble. The chart shows the post-pandemic rebound as stocks surged due to massive liquidity injections inflating the ratio yet again.

Since then, we've seen a relatively steady ratio until the drop in recent weeks.

Bubbles Pop

The relationship between Federal Reserve monetary policy and the Dow-to-Gold ratio underscores an important fundamental -- the stock market is subject to inflation just like consumer prices. In fact, monetary inflation often shows up in equities first. In a nutshell, central bank money creation tends to blow up asset bubbles.

In effect, this means that while stocks have surged in nominal terms, they have made much less progress in real (inflation-adjusted) returns.

I have been saying for years that the stock market is extremely overvalued, thanks to the Fed's monetary malfeasance. It is a bubble waiting for a pin. It started to deflate in 2018, but the Fed was able to use the pandemic as an excuse to pump the air back in. Now, it may have found its pin in Trump tariffs.

There is little question that the stock market is extremely overvalued. In December, CurrentMarketValuation.com called the S&P 500 "strongly overvalued" based on the price-earnings ratio.

If history is any indication, the sudden drop in the Dow-to-Gold ratio could be signaling the beginning of a massive selloff in stock and a major rally in gold.

An Opportunity for Investors

The recent breakdown in the Dow-to-gold ratio may signal that it's time to consider getting out of stocks and into gold.

Even if Trump backs off the aggressive tariffs and provides some relief for markets, it won't change the fact that stocks are overvalued. We still haven't dealt with the ramifications of well over a decade of artificially low interest rates and multiple rounds of QE.

We will.

Consider this: if the Dow-gold ratio were to revisit the 1-1, we’d be looking at either a massive stock market crash, an explosive move higher in precious metals prices, or some combination of both.

While a return to a 1-1 ratio would be extreme, even a more modest contraction of the ratio presents an opportunity. If the ratio returned to 5-1, we'd be looking at $7,500 gold if there was no corresponding drop in the Dow. If the Dow fell to 20,000, gold would still be over $4,000 an ounce.

And don't forget that silver typically outperforms gold in the latter part of a gold bull market. With the gold-to-silver ratio over 100-1, we know that silver is significantly undervalued compared to gold and set up for a major gain. So this extremely high gold-to-silver ratio suggests one might be overweight to silver in comparison to gold.

Nobody has a crystal ball. Trump could relent on tariffs tomorrow and set off a relief rally in stocks. But it won't change the fact that the market is a "big, fat, ugly bubble," to quote Donald Trump in 2016. Smart investors are positioned for that reality.

Original Post

data.title: Skyward Specialty: A Company With A Niche Strategy
data.description:
Skyward Specialty Insurance (NASDAQ:SKWD) has been rated as a Strong Buy stock for over a year now. I decided to research the stock since it has an excellent momentum grade, 9 upward revisions in the last 90 days, and sustainable growth potential.

SKWD focuses on niche areas of insurance coverage such as professional lines, surety, captivity, and agriculture. In total, the company operates in 8 divisions to date. What really caught my attention with SKWD is their approach to pricing insurance premium rates. Their use of technology and advanced analytics is brilliant, which is why I believe that SKWD is a good buy with good growth prospects in the future. After all, since its IPO in 2023, the stock has more than tripled from its IPO price, as shown below:

The "Rule Our Niche" Effect

One of the major issues that arose with the LA wildfires was the ability to incorporate catastrophic CAT losses in their premium rates. I recently covered Mercury General (MCY) and discussed the modeling of catastrophic losses in premium rates. MCY's expected losses will be larger than $150 Mn retention of its $1.29 Bn CAT reinsurance program. As a result of this, MCY might have issues with its reinsurance renewal treaties come July 2025. You can read the article here.

Moving on to SKWD, the company experienced more than 3 CAT events in 2024 due to losses attributed to Hurricane Milton, Hurricane Helene, and Hurricane Beryl in the 3rd quarter of 2024. In addition to these events, there is an anticipated $10 Mn dent to be incurred from the LA wildfires. When you look at the occurrence of these events, MCY suffered a huge dip of more than 20% when the wildfires occurred. However, SKWD hasn't had that kind of steep reaction; in fact, the stock went on to appreciate more since 2Q2024.

In the latest earnings call, Skywest reported $1.74 Bn in gross written premium (GWP), which is a 19.4% increase from last year's value. Cumulatively, the 8 divisions are now sustainable enough to contribute over $100 Mn to the overall earnings of the business, as shown below:

Our BUY Thesis

Growth

Skywest has managed to run its 8 divisions profitably, and they seem to have control of their expansion plans. In the diagram above, it is clear that all their business segments are contributing to the GWP. This means that even when management expands into other segments such as media, life sciences, mortgage, credit, and renewable energy, SKWD will manage to turn these business divisions and drive them into profitability, as noted by the CEO:

Throughout 2024 we continued to thoughtfully diversify our product portfolio, strategically launching new units including Media Liability, Life Sciences, Mortgage and Credit, and Renewable Energy. Our focus and disciplined execution of our "Rule Our Niche" strategy, and the extraordinary efforts of my 600 plus colleagues made 2024 another impressive year for our Company, and we are confident that we have built the foundation that will propel us in 2025 and beyond.

As far as I am concerned, their growth ambitions are justified, sustainable, and manageable given the previous track record they've had. SKWD SA's Quant growth grade stands at B, which I would say corresponds with their growth plans. Previously, the increase in GWP in 2024 was driven by surety and captivity divisions, which contributed the largest portion of 52.6% and 43.1%, respectively.

Source: SKWD Investor 4Q2024 Presentation

Skywest has been delivering decent underwriting results with low underwriting volatility during claim payouts. The company has a low adjusted combined ratio, as shown below. The fact that management mentioned shifting struggling businesses into more profitable lines shows that its management is very keen on only running profitable businesses. Which makes me believe that their expansion into the proposed segments will definitely be a success. Despite the 3 hurricanes in 2024 and the LA wildfires, SKWD has managed to maintain its catastrophic losses under 2% and its expense ratio under the guided 30%.

Profitability

SKWD's D profitability grade is a bit on the lower end. However, in my opinion, it is justified. After the CAT events in 2024, the 3 hurricanes put a temporary hole in their books, which affected their Q3 and Q4 earnings. I wouldn't worry so much about their profitability grade given the 2024 events. In fact, in my opinion, this shows the quality of their book, its resilience, and its ability to manage catastrophic losses efficiently.

The CEO noted that:

The loss ratios for the fourth quarter and year ended 2024 increased 5.8 points and 1.3 points, respectively, when compared to the same 2023 periods, primarily due to the net impact of prior accident year development related to the LPT. The fourth quarter and year ended 2024 were also impacted by higher catastrophe losses, primarily from Hurricane Milton in the fourth quarter of 2024 and Hurricanes Helene and Beryl in the third quarter of 2024....

Earnings

From an earnings perspective, the most important factor is that analysts have started looking at the stock from a positive angle. In the last 90 days, SKWD has had 9 upward revisions, compared to only 1 down revision. From its earnings, SKWD has been beating consensus estimate since 2022.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, a couple of things stand out:

  • SKWD is trading at a forward EV/Sales of 1.51x, representing a 45.94% discount to its sector median.
  • SKWD is trading at a forward Price/Sales of 1.73x, a 36.91% discount from its sector median.
  • In comparison with its peers, SKWD's price/sales stands at 1.73x, while Hamilton Insurance (HG) trades at 0.87x, Lemonade (LMND) trades at 3.80x, Stewart (STC) trades at 0.74x, and Fidelis Insurance (FIHL) and Root (ROOT) trade at 0.74x and 1.42x, respectively.

SKWD Key Risks

The biggest risk for SKWD is the repeat of another catastrophic event that dents their book. While the last CAT events in 2024 were not that huge, should Skywest experience an event as massive as what Mercury General is currently facing, then such could adversely affect their profitability, which could also affect their abilities to pay the claims, trigger its reinsurance limits, and negatively affect the overall core business. Having said that, SKWD has so far managed to keep its exposure level at acceptable limits. This is through diversification of their insurance products, use of technology and advanced analytics in pricing their rates, and their capital allocation strategy in their investment portfolio (65% is invested in fixed income securities).

Final Thoughts

Skywest is a small company that recently listed its stock. Since its IPO price of $15.00 per share, the company has more than tripled its share price in 3 years' time. Despite this rise, I believe SKWD has a lot more to offer for long-term investors. As we have seen, SKWD has managed to turn the 8 divisions into profitability, with each contributing over $100 Mn to the overall $1.74 Bn in GWP. Management has also assured of a "Rule Our Niche" effect, even as they plan to venture into other divisions such as life sciences and media. SKWD has also had a taste of what CAT losses could look like and what it could do to their books, and in my opinion, they did handle 2024 very well. While there was a decrease in profitability in Q3 and Q4, there is definitely a lot more to desire from this company.

data.title: BIL ETF: Safeguard Your Assets From Extreme Volatility
data.description:
Amid the ongoing macro uncertainty, the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (NYSEARCA:BIL) could be a good hedge to the current market volatility with a potential to deliver up to 4% yield in my view over the next 12 months. My first coverage on BIL was back in two years, where I supposed the fund to earn a moderate return, providing a hedge to the interest rate risk. The ETF is currently yielding 4.8% on a TTM basis, and I believe that BIL is a reasonable instrument to overcome the recent market panic. The latter is caused by increased volatility associated with fears of mirror tariffs effects on the U.S. and global economies.

Fund overview

In my previous piece on BIL in mid-2023, I wrote that it’s reasonable to expect a 4% yield for the fund to earn over a 12-month period. The fund exhibited a stability of this income outlook, reaching the target, and is now boasting a 4.8% TTM yield.

Before we move forward, let’s recall the essence of the fund with the following quote from the previous article:

The SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF exposes its investors to U.S. Treasury bills with maturities ranging from one to three months. BIL tracks the Bloomberg 1-3 Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index, which includes all publicly issued T-bills that have the respective maturities and are rated investment-grade.

The fund’s portfolio consists of 17 Treasury bills with a total AUM of $42.1 billion, growing by circa $13 billion over the last two years. The fund is the largest amid the ultra-short U.S. government bond ETFs and features a duration of just 0.15 years.

It’s obvious that the market saturation in recent years has been quite favorable to the fund’s investors, where the yield picked up significantly due to the elevated interest rate environment.

Following three consecutive interest rate cuts, the Fed took a break at the January 2025 meeting, keeping the range at 4.25-4.50%. The central bank officials seem hesitant to rush with further rate cuts in the context of strong economic growth, a stable labor market, and accelerating inflation. Still, the futures suggest that the main scenario remains further rate cuts, where the economic decisions of the new administration are becoming a new factor of uncertainty. According to Fitch Ratings, the effective US tariff rate on all imports has soared to 22% from 2.5% in 2024 due to the new global levies. The 2018 Fed model stipulates that each 1% increase in the tariff rate lowers GDP by 0.14% and contributes to core PCE inflation by 0.09%. This could imply up to a 2.8% loss in GDP and an additional 1.8% increase in prices, which is likely to occur over the next few years. As a result, the market expectation for the Fed’s interest rate trajectory has been reassessed. The Fed futures currently price four cuts by 25 bps over the next 12 months.

However, there is still no full clarity on the resulting economic impact of the new administration’s changes in the tariffs, immigration, regulation, and fiscal policy areas. The Fed may face a difficult choice between its inflation and maximum employment targets, as raising tariffs is expected to lead to a jump in inflation and a decline in economic growth and employment. We can also see from the following chart that the BIL’s net inflows surpassed $1 billion following the "Liberation Day" and tariffs announcement.

At the same time, the net inflows during the last 3 months reached $6 billion, which suggests that investors may anticipate a protracted and uncertain interest rate patch. Perhaps, when the real signs of a recession and a threatening rise in unemployment appear, the central bank could proceed to actively reduce the interest rate. Meanwhile, the labor market remains balanced, where the March 2025 unemployment rate stood at 4.2%. A combination of low layoffs, muted job growth, and slowing labor force growth has kept the unemployment rate broadly stable. What’s more is that the economy remains in good shape, which means that the main focus of the central bank official should fall on inflation.

Risk factors

The fund provides exposure to T-bills, which are deemed to be reasonably safe assets. However, the consequences of the new tariffs standoff add a new layer of macroeconomic uncertainty. In case of a recession, the US central bank could resort to sharper interest rate cuts, which could decrease the investment return of the fund’s investors.

Investment conclusion

Taking the current market expectation regarding the interest rates over the next 12 months, the average rate is still around 3.5%. However, I will maintain my bullish thesis on BIL amid the growing macro uncertainty caused by new tariff conditions. The consequences of the emerging trade tensions remain unclear, which could further intensify the ongoing market volatility. In this context, I find the BIL ETF to be a reasonable investment over the next 12 months ahead, as the fund is entirely determined by the level of interest rates and practically independent of their movements. In my view, BIL provides a good opportunity to overcome the current market volatility and earn up to a 4% return in a one-year horizon.

While this has negatively impacted Microchip’s near-term financials, we believe that its large and fragmented customer base (~120,000 customers and several thousand SKUs [stock keeping units]) and sticky products will allow it to maintain pricing and market share once a recovery materializes. We took advantage of the weakness to add to our position, confident that Microchip is a well-positioned, scaled player in the analog/microcontroller market with the potential for higher margins in the long term.

Outlook & Strategy

  • As the US political landscape shifts, we are reminded why we try to own resilient, durable franchises that can withstand various uncertainties and generate attractive risk-adjusted returns over the long term. We anticipate a potentially more business-friendly environment under the new administration in the coming four years, which would support economic growth and a broadening of the market. The ISM Manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index has been in contraction territory for some time; should that trend reverse, capital that has been on the sidelines may find more incentive to enter the market. We believe there are pockets that would benefit, perhaps those overlooked in the narrow market of 2023 and 2024, more than others. data.title: Sharp Pullback - Start Building Your Income Portfolio: Realty Income
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

"The mistake is thinking that there can be an antidote to the uncertainty"

– David Levithan

Throughout much of our lives, it can be a common theme that individuals are looking for a sense of stability and calm. It's rare to find a person who enjoys and thrives in consistent uncertainty or instability. Some may claim to have the ability to thrive in those environments, but they can only do so for so long before they are also overwhelmed.

The economy and especially the stock market dislike uncertainty. Throughout history, the United States of America has had strong relationships with different countries around the world, with decades of trade and political ties. Yet, in recent years, those ties and those trade agreements have been strained because of political uncertainty and a growing sense of unfairness in the mind of the American public. America has been a nation marked by great generosity, helping rebuild Europe after World War Two, and donating millions of dollars to countries worldwide to help them become more established. However, this shouldn't be seen as simply an act of philanthropy on the part of America.

Historically, the U.S. has engaged in dollar diplomacy. This is where, instead of using their military might to achieve what they want, they will throw money at a problem, or throw money at a government, or throw money at a country to get what they want – in essence, buying them out. Over the years, the dollar amount it takes to get what they want has risen, and now the current administration no longer feels that this type of diplomacy is effective. The current administration is taking a different approach. Instead of deciding to play dollar diplomacy, they are cutting off the spigot and focusing on the home-front.

Historically, the stock market has performed the strongest when the government within the United States has been split between Democrats and Republicans, meaning that no party has a majority. This causes the government to be unable to pass many laws and to be exceptionally unproductive. This unproductive nature creates more calm and certainty, allowing business owners and operators and the stock market itself to have a better chance to predict what may come next and be able to develop and invest accordingly.

The biggest problem that an investor can have in the current market is thinking that there is a simple solution or a one-shot antidote to the uncertainty that is currently going on. Many investors will simply pull out of the stock market, selling common equity and moving on to safer shores like Treasury notes, Mortgage-backed securities, or other types of investments that are considered safe havens during periods of uncertainty and economic weakness. This can be effective as a means of capital preservation, accepting a significantly lower return in the short term to try and prevent losses. However, it's those who are willing to embrace greater volatility that often see outsized gains in the end. It's been famously said that far more money has been lost by investors trying to anticipate corrections than has been lost in all the corrections combined. An investor who tries to sell out on every dip will have significantly lower returns than an investor who simply stays put.

Today, I want to look at an income investment that could be considered as much of a safe haven as any other investment, but allows you to remain exposed to the common equity markets, providing you with the potential to see great upside as well as great income.

Let's dive in!

A Bastion of Calm In a Sea Of Uncertainty

Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O), yielding 5.8% is a REIT that labels itself as "The Monthly Dividend Company" and has been a reliable stalwart for dividend investors for many decades. O recently announced its 130th dividend increase since being listed on the NYSE in 1994. It has now increased its dividends for 111 consecutive quarters. We have frequently covered this blue-chip company.

It is a funny thing when O raises its dividend, as many investors will laugh at the size of the raises, which are frequently quite small. The CAGR of O's dividend raises has been 4.3%. Source

Yet, these "small" increases occurring very frequently have added up. Long-term investors have enjoyed the power of compounding being applied to their income.

O has a defensive business model. It buys properties and collects rent. O relies on long-term triple-net leases, in which the tenant is responsible for most property-level expenses.

Since being listed, O has had only one year where its AFFO (Adjusted Funds From Operations) declined, and that was in 2009. Source

Overall, O has experienced average AFFO/share growth of over 5%. O's consistency makes it a top pick for stocks we would want to hold through a recession. We can have a lot of confidence that its earnings have held up through the Dot-com bust, the GFC, and COVID. That has translated to dividend growth, even during the toughest times.

To top it off, O is trading at a very low valuation today. Like many REITs, the market has sold it off in the face of higher interest rates, and over the past two years, it has traded at valuations similar to where it was trading in the heart of the COVID crisis:

With an A- rated balance sheet, higher interest rates are at most a mild inconvenience for O's operations. O will continue doing what it has been doing for over 30 years – growing its earnings and raising its dividend.

The opportunity to invest in a REIT that has such a strong track record and high level of safety with an initial yield of 5.8% is a gift from the market. With an uncertain future for the economy, we want companies that have a high probability of providing us with income growth regardless of what happens.

Conclusion

O is a REIT with a market-beating legacy. Its future outlook is expected to be similar – steady dividend growth, unexciting earnings growth, and quiet market-beating returns over the long haul. When market worries rise, it's nice and comforting to see my O shares happily producing income month after month.

Instead of trying to quell the craziness in the market, accept it as part of the experience. The market will always have something it's panicking about and something it's celebrating – these days, it's tariffs and AI. In the past, it was Websites and Y2K. If you let others govern your timing, you'll never invest.

Instead, collect steady income and enjoy the dividends pouring in from defensive value-oriented dividend stocks, and your retirement will be marked by calm, tranquility, and serenity – at least on the financial front!

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Dividends Over Drama: Here's What Smart Money Is Buying
data.description:
Introduction

Have you ever heard of the cash trap?

Last year, I wrote a number of articles on this phenomenon, including one on October 30, which had the title "A $6.8 Trillion Cash Trap? 2 Of My Favorite Dividend Picks For What's Next."

Essentially, the cash trap is based on the fact that elevated short-term interest rates caused Americans to push trillions into money market funds, as they could get rates of 5% and more by simply holding risk-free government debt.

This was the result of the post-pandemic tightening cycle of the Federal Reserve, which made it much more attractive for many retirees to buy short-term government debt.

Moreover, despite some rate cuts last year, short-term debt is still attractive for many, as the chart from the New York Fed below shows.

With that said, the concept of the 'trap' was based on easing inflation and potentially longer-term rate cuts. These would have lowered money market fund rates and forced investors to look for income in places like the stock market.

For now, that hasn't happened, as the effective Federal Reserve rate is still north of 4.0%, a number a lot of people are perfectly comfortable with.

On the contrary.

These investments are not a trap but a total blessing!

As we all know by now, markets are in turmoil. Hence, it helps that "Americans Are Sitting on a Cash Pile as Stocks Reel," as The Wall Street Journal wrote in the title of a recent article.

As you may have guessed, the title refers to money market funds, which have ballooned to a stunning $7.4 trillion, $600 billion more than when I wrote the October article I just mentioned.

Moreover, to put things in perspective, Americans have close to a fifth of their assets in cash, as The Journal reports:

Some analysts say that Americans have simply shifted their assets into higher-yielding money markets from traditional bank accounts, and expect that cash to stay put. [...]

Cash is still paying higher yields than before the pandemic, even with the Fed’s rate cuts last year. The average return on money-market funds is at 4.2%, down from 4.3% in December, Crane data show. In March, individual investors raised the share of cash in their portfolios to about 18.3%, up from 17.4% the prior month, according to the American Association of Individual Investors. - WSJ (emphasis added)

For now, I agree with the article when it comes to expectations of this money staying put. As we can see below, 'nobody' is bullish, as AAII data shows that roughly 62% of all surveyed retail investors are bearish. That's twice the historical average.

The good news is that investors have an edge. While I doubt that anyone is enjoying this market carnage, the fact that most go into it with a decent cash position is fantastic news.

After all, who doesn't like buying the dip?

Here's What Smart Money Is Buying

In recent days, I have written a lot about the stocks that I am buying, and I will obviously continue to do so.

The problem is that I am currently (not in general) mainly buying dividend growth stocks with somewhat subdued yields, a strategy that works for me but not for many of my readers who require higher income.

So, as I promised, this article is a way to provide food for thought for a bigger audience.

In this article, I'm using some thoughts from 'smart money,' which were brought up in a Bloomberg article titled "What to Do With Your Money Right Now as Tariffs Reshape Markets."

As many opinions align with my own research, it provides a great framework for a wide range of investors.

For starters, a good start is to simply reassess one's portfolio. What do you own? Why did you buy it?

If you, like me, invested in long-term dividend stocks with a focus on resilient business models and a healthy balance sheet, odds are you don't have to make any (major) changes.

Especially investors who prefer index funds are in a great spot to do absolutely nothing. After all, one of the biggest factors that tends to derail our investment strategies is our consistent need to 'do something.'

After all, time in the market beats timing the market.

This doesn't just sound catchy, but it's also backed by decades worth of data.

As we can see in the chart below:

  • Since 1950, on a one-year rolling basis, stocks have been a casino. Nobody knows what the next 12 months look like. This includes billionaires, retail investors, and me.
  • On a 10-year rolling basis, the worst return for stock-only investors was basically flat.
  • Investors who invest in 60/40 portfolios have never lost money on a five-year rolling basis.
  • On a 20-year rolling basis, stock investors have never lost money.

It's worth noting that these numbers encompass the Great Financial Crisis, the pandemic, and every crash and rally since the end of the Second World War. That's quite a substantial data sample.

Having said that, as you can imagine, 'buying the dip' remains one of Wall Street's favorite activities - and rightfully so.

Instead of just telling investors to deploy capital, I prefer the approach of Tiger 21 founder Michael Sonnenfeldt. He brings up a strategy I have also brought up in the past, which is regularly investing during volatile periods and increasing investment as stock prices decline. I added emphasis to the quote below:

A smart move investors can make right now, he said, is to put a systematic strategy in place to increase buying as markets fall, either directly or through ETFs. “Investors can move in stages by investing a set amount when the index drops 20% from its high, double that amount at 25%, and increase further at 30%," said Sonnenfeldt. That “allows investors to take advantage of market volatility without being driven by fear, and to execute a smart offense in a downturn with a strategy that can pay off in the long term.” - Bloomberg

Others are making the case for high-quality income, which is an area I have liked since I started to build my 'growth-to-value' rotation thesis after the pandemic.

In this case, Emily Roland, the co-chief investment strategist at John Hancock Investment Management, makes the case for investment-grade corporate bonds. These are corporate bonds with a credit rating of at least BBB-.

Generally speaking, I don't like the debt market. That's mainly because I like to invest in equity. I want to own a piece of a company for decades. I want to benefit through capital gains and dividend growth, not be a mere provider of debt.

Currently, we're seeing some cracks, as global economic uncertainty has damaged the debt market a bit. As we can see below, the amount of distressed debt (trading below 80% of its par value) has made a comeback.

That's why I wrote, "High-yield debt is a great place for income - until it isn't."

The good news when buying investment-grade corporate debt is that you're lending money to companies like JPMorgan (JPM), Bank of America (BAC), Oracle (ORCL), UnitedHealth (UNH), and others.

These companies are the largest issuers in the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), which currently yields roughly 4.5%. Roughly half of the debt in that ETF has an A-range rating. The other half has a BBB-range rating.

Over the past ten years, LQD has been a much better investment than the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), which tracks long-term government bonds.

My personal opinion has been for a while that I would rather lend money to an A-rated American corporation with a wide moat than the U.S. government, as I simply prefer the risk/reward.

Moreover, we indeed see a decline in longer-term bond rates, allowing the U.S. Treasury to refinance maturing debt and extend average maturities (as I discussed in this article). I believe corporate bonds are not a bad place to be.

Needless to say, bonds usually don't keep up with stocks. Only buy corporate bonds to add some safety to your portfolio. For younger investors, stocks are usually the best place to be.

Except during recessions. That's when (investment-grade) bonds are the place to be for income and safety.

The next idea is right up my alley, as it's something I spend most of my time on. I'm talking about 'opportunistic plays,' which John Pantekidis, the managing partner of TwinFocus, is focusing on.

What sounds quite fancy is nothing more than a term for stocks that bring something special to the table. Mr. Pantekidis, for example, is buying defense and nuclear stocks, two areas with strong secular growth.

The defense industry is an area I have dedicated a lot of energy to in recent years. In fact, I own five major players in the aerospace and defense industry. I covered all of them in a March 16 article titled "Defending Dividends: Why American Defense Stocks Are The Ultimate Value Play."

I explained why I remain super bullish despite defense spending fears, new disruptors, and global uncertainty. My favorite deep-value play in this area is Lockheed Martin (LMT), which currently yields 3.0%. It generates almost no money from commercial operations and has raised its dividend for more than 20 consecutive years.

Currently trading at just 18.3x earnings, I see a conservative path to >14% annual returns through at least 2027.

Other opportunistic plays with limited risk are midstream companies, the owners of pipelines, storage, and gathering/processing facilities used by oil and gas producers.

Unlike their customers, midstream companies do not care much about the price of oil, gas, and related commodities. To them, it does not matter if oil trades at $60 or $90, as almost all money comes from take-or-pay and long-term contracted operations with consistent inflation escalators.

They benefit from secular growth like rising export demand for North American energy, decades of underinvestment in new pipelines, and the (related) fact that most have shifted their focus from investing in CapEx to growing free cash flow.

However, recent tariff headlines have sent shockwaves through the industry, as even high-quality stocks like ONEOK (OKE), Antero Midstream (AM), Kinder Morgan (KMI), MPLX (MPLX), and others sold off violently. Please note that MPLX is a Master Limited Partnership. All other midstream companies I just mentioned are C-Corps, which do NOT issue K-1 forms.

I believe these sell-offs have two reasons:

  • General panic is causing investors to take profits, while others are forced to cover margin calls. In other words, when markets go south with a ton of volatility, people do not always tend to make the smartest decisions.
  • China announced retaliation. Markets fear that general export demand for key commodities like natural gas liquids will suffer.

While these stock price drawdowns aren't fun, I like them, as I believe fears are overblown. While a recession could temporarily lower demand for energy, the United States will remain a key player in energy. Tariffs won't change that.

For example, the U.S. is the largest exporter of liquefied natural gas, a commodity that most nations simply cannot produce in large quantities.

The same goes for natural gas liquids like propane, which nations like China need a lot of for a wide range of purposes. Over the past ten years, exports of this commodity have grown by more than 4x.

I will be adding to Antero Midstream this week, as I love its 5.5% yield. I am also looking into ONEOK, which I want to buy for my parents. Whereas Antero operates in Appalachia, ONEOK has a bigger footprint in the Permian, where it grows through areas like natural gas liquids and refined products.

If I could, I would also buy MPLX, as I LOVE its business model and its 8% yield. However, because I am European, I cannot invest in MLPs.

Another stock I want to bring up is S&P Global (SPGI), the world's largest rating agency, which has spent decades diversifying its business model. Although the company yields just 0.9%, I wanted to bring it up, as it's one of the companies that's rarely 'cheap.'

One reason is its margin profile, as it has a company-wide operating margin of 49%, one of the highest among service companies. It also benefits from a massive wave of maturing debt. Most of this debt will require a rating to attract a wide pool of buyers.

Over the next 84 months, close to $16 trillion in debt will mature.

Although current tariff fears have muddied the water, a decline in longer-term rates could pave a very favorable way for even higher issuance, potentially making SPGI one of the big winners of the next bull market.

Now turning to the 2024 issuance environment. We saw nearly $4 trillion in billed issuance in 2024, significantly outpacing our expectations, with extremely favorable market conditions, including tight credit spreads and lowering interest rates. We saw many issuers take advantage in 2024 to refinance debt and felt very strong activity in CLO volumes as well as repricing and amend and extend activity. - SPGI 4Q24 Earnings Call

Currently trading at 28x earnings with an annual EPS growth outlook of 10-12%, I believe SPGI is a great buy if markets continue to weaken, as it's a great low-yield 'opportunistic' dividend grower.

Needless to say, as this week progresses and the tariff situation unfolds, we'll discuss a lot more opportunities.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

As it turns out, the cash trap isn't a trap, it's a lifeline.

With $7.4 trillion parked in money markets and bearish sentiment at twice the historical average, having dry powder is a blessing for both the market and individual investors.

The good news is that while markets are volatile, history shows that patience wins, as stocks haven't lost money over 20-year periods, and 60/40 portfolios have never been negative after five years.

With that said, for income, investment-grade corporate bonds (like LQD) offer more attractive yields than treasuries. Meanwhile, opportunistic plays like defense stocks, midstream, and even S&P Global, offer attractive risk/rewards.

In general, I like wide-moat dividend stocks, including higher-yielding players and lower-yielding dividend growers. That's why we will continue to discuss new opportunities in the days, weeks, and months ahead.

So, what's the bottom line?

Stay calm, reassess your portfolio, and buy the dip systematically.

And, as always, time in the market beats timing the market, especially when fear is this high.

Risks To My Thesis

To me, the biggest risk is a scenario where inflation remains sticky, preventing the Fed from easing rates and long-term bond yields from falling. This would increase recession and even stagflation risks.
